title: Document

child_sections.title: Import tax expenditure report
child_sections.summary: The "Import Tax Expenditure Report" from Ethiopia's Ministry of Finance (Tax Policy Directorate) analyzes the financial impact of tax exemptions and preferential treatments related to imports over fiscal years 2018/19 to 2020/21. Published in September 2022, the report quantifies these "tax expenditures"—the revenue foregone due to specific import-related policies—providing insights into their cost to the government. It serves as a key document for evaluating the efficiency and effectiveness of Ethiopia's trade tax system.

child_sections.title: 2 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The FY 2018/19 – 2020/21 Import Tax Expenditure Report was collaboratively produced by the IFS Centre for Tax Analysis in Developing Countries (TaxDev) team—Daniel Prinz, Edris Seid and Ben Waltmann—and the Ethiopian Ministry of Finance’s Tax Policy Directorate, led by Mulay Weldu. The report analyzes import tax expenditures over a three-year period within Ethiopia. It represents a joint effort between international tax experts (TaxDev) and domestic policymakers at the Ministry of Finance.

child_sections.title: 3 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The "Import Tax Expenditure Report" covers the fiscal years 2018/19 through 2020/21 and begins with an executive summary followed by an introduction. The report likely details financial impacts of tax concessions or exemptions related to imports within Australia, though specific findings aren't provided in this section excerpt. It is produced by the Australian Border Force, as indicated by its placement within their broader documentation.

child_sections.title: 4 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The FY 2018/19 – 2020/21 import tax expenditure report focuses on analyzing reductions to tax liabilities for individuals, firms, and other entities due to specific tax provisions. This exercise is considered crucial for effective public financial management by providing insight into the cost of these preferential tax treatments. The report aims to understand and estimate the value of these "tax expenditures" within the import sector.

child_sections.title: 5 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report for FY 2018/19 – 2020/21 reveals that import tax expenditures represented approximately 16-20% of the total import value over those three years. In FY 2020/21, these expenditures were particularly significant for **motor vehicles** and **animal & vegetable fats**, collectively accounting for nearly 40% of all import tax expenditure that year. This indicates a substantial impact of tax concessions on these specific goods categories.

child_sections.title: 6 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The FY 2018/19 – 2020/21 import tax expenditure report estimates federal revenue losses from import taxes in Ethiopia. These estimates are calculated using detailed shipment-level data encompassing customs duties, excise duty, VAT, and surtaxes collected on imported goods. The report covers a three-year period to provide insights into trends within the Ethiopian import taxation system.

child_sections.title: 7 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: Ethiopia’s government utilized tax expenditures – including holidays, credits, and preferential rates – from FY 2018/19 to 2020/21 to stimulate investment growth. These policies were specifically targeted towards encouraging expansion within certain sectors and for particular producers across the country. The report focuses on the rationale behind these measures and identifies associated issues with their implementation.

child_sections.title: 8 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The Ethiopian government utilized import tax expenditure measures from FY 2018/19 – 2020/21 to encourage economic transformation towards industrialization and improve living standards. However, a key issue identified is a lack of transparency regarding the actual amount of tax revenue being forgone through these expenditures. Despite legal requirements, reporting on these foregone revenues remains insufficient.

child_sections.title: 9 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report for FY 2018/19 – 2020/21 focuses on quantifying deviations from a defined "benchmark tax system" to calculate tax expenditure amounts. This report begins by outlining the crucial first step in this process: establishing what constitutes that baseline benchmark system against which import taxes are measured. The document doesn't detail specific expenditure figures, but rather explains the foundational methodology used for their calculation.

child_sections.title: 10 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The FY 2018/19 – 2020/21 import tax expenditure report indicates that these exemptions are projected to increase overall tax revenue despite reducing direct import taxes. Specifically, reduced import VAT is expected to boost domestic VAT collection on finished products, and all import tax expenditures likely contribute to higher income tax collections. This suggests a net positive impact on total government revenue through broader economic effects.

child_sections.title: 11 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: Ethiopia’s import tax expenditure report (FY 2018/19 – 2020/21) highlights significant revenue foregone due to duty-free access granted to **Sudan** under a bilateral trade agreement, with customs duties benchmarked at zero for Sudanese goods. Additionally, the report notes exemptions provided to **international organisations** and their foreign personnel. This resulted in lost potential tax revenue during the specified fiscal years.

child_sections.title: 12 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report for FY 2018/19 – 2020/21 details significant tax exemptions granted on **basic food items** like milk, bread, and grains, as well as **agricultural inputs** including fertilizers and seeds. These exemptions are managed within the framework of Ethiopia’s **benchmark system for VAT on imports**. The report likely analyzes the financial impact (tax expenditure) resulting from these specific import concessions.

child_sections.title: 13 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The FY 2018/19 – 2020/21 Import Tax Expenditure Report defines the benchmark for surtax on imports as being levied on **importers** (businesses or individuals) based on the sum of the **CIF value of imports, customs duty, and excise tax payable**. This report establishes the foundational elements for calculating potential revenue loss due to import-related tax expenditures within this period. It does not detail expenditure amounts, only the *basis* upon which they are calculated.

child_sections.title: 14 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: This report analyzes import tax expenditure from FY 2018/19 to 2020/21, focusing on the difference between benchmark customs duty rates (𝜏𝑏𝑐) and actual charged rates (𝜏𝑎𝑐) for individual shipments (𝑖𝑡). The analysis utilizes Cost, Insurance and Freight (CIF) values of imports (𝐶𝐼𝐹) to quantify these tax expenditure amounts. Essentially, it measures the revenue foregone due to deviations from standard import duty levels.

child_sections.title: 15 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report for FY 2018/19 – 2020/21 utilizes detailed shipment-level data from the **Ethiopian Customs Commission** at the eight-digit HS code level. The report analyzes import trends over these three fiscal years, coinciding with a transition period where the Commission moved away from the **ASYCUDA** system for customs data management. This data provides insights into potential revenue loss due to various import tax provisions within Ethiopia.

child_sections.title: 16 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report for FY 2018/19 – 2020/21 estimates total tax expenditures at ETB 120.7 billion in FY 2020/21. This figure comprises ETB 38.9 billion in lost customs duty, ETB 18.8 billion in excise duty, and ETB 44.6 billion in other forgone taxes. The report quantifies the financial impact of tax incentives and exemptions on Ethiopia's import revenue during this three-year period.

child_sections.title: 17 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The Ethiopian Import Tax Expenditure Report for FY 2018/19 – 2020/21 estimates tax losses due to exemptions and other incentives between ETB 68.7 billion and ETB 99.3 billion. These losses represented 2.6% - 3.7% of Ethiopia’s GDP and approximately 20% of the total import value during that period, with figures potentially impacted by a transition in customs IT systems. The report highlights significant revenue implications for the Ethiopian government related to import policies.

child_sections.title: 18 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report for FY 2018/19 – 2020/21 in Ethiopia shows tax expenditures related to imports, measured in Ethiopian Birr (ETB million). Notably, these expenditures increased in FY 2020/21 primarily due to higher excise taxes on vehicles boosting the value of capital goods exemptions within that sector. The report details a breakdown of tax expenditure types over the three fiscal years, linked to the total CIF value of imports.

child_sections.title: 19 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report for FY 2018/19 – 2020/21, measured in Ethiopian Birr (ETB million), shows a significant rise in tax expenditures on both animal and vegetable products. Specifically, the import value of animal and vegetable fats more than tripled between 2019/20 and 2020/21, with animal product expenditures increasing from 139.41 ETB million to 298.65 ETB million over that period. The report details these changes across broad commodity categories as shown in Table 4.2.

child_sections.title: 20 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report for FY 2018/19 – 2020/21 reveals that **animal or vegetable fats** accounted for the largest share of tax expenditures (**48.1%**) during this period. This was a significant increase compared to FY 2019/20, where **prepared food and beverages (including sugar)** held the highest proportion. The report details these expenditures as percentages of total import value across broad commodity categories (as shown in Table 4.3).

child_sections.title: 21 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report for FY2018/19 – 2020/21 estimates overall tax expenditures as broadly consistent with prior assessments, despite some variation. These differences largely stem from how the "benchmark tax system" is defined, as detailed in Box 4.1. The report doesn't specify which entities benefit or the specific amounts of expenditure beyond noting they are comparable to previous years.

child_sections.title: 22 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report for FY 2018/19 – 2020/21 analyzes tax revenue foregone due to various import concessions and exemptions, with figures presented both across all customs IT systems ("this report") and specifically using the eCMS system ("this report, eCMS only"). The report uses standard ECC rates as a benchmark for calculating these expenditure estimates. It's important to note that headline figures may not account for double-counting of shipments due to data limitations across different systems.

child_sections.title: 23 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: Between FY 2018/19 and FY 2020/21, Ethiopia experienced significant customs duty expenditures totaling around ETB 23 billion overall, with a peak of ETB 30 billion in FY 2018/19. These expenditures were comprised of both direct effects and indirect effects stemming from surtaxes (as illustrated in Figure 4.3). The report analyzes these figures to understand the financial impact of duty concessions over this three-year period.

child_sections.title: 24 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report for FY 2018/19 – 2020/21 shows that customs duties represented approximately 5-6% of total imports during those years. These customs duty tax expenditures comprised around 30% of *all* tax expenditures reported, indicating a relatively smaller portion of overall tax relief compared to other areas. The report utilizes Figure 4.4 to illustrate these shares.

child_sections.title: 25 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report for FY 2018/19 – 2020/21 (Table 4.5) details customs duty tax expenditures in Ethiopian Birr (ETB million), broken down by exemption reductions, indirect effects through excise, VAT and surtax, and higher-order effects. The report quantifies these various forms of import tax relief, providing a comprehensive overview of foregone revenue due to different policy mechanisms impacting customs duties. It doesn't provide specific values but outlines the *types* of expenditures analyzed by the Ethiopian government.

child_sections.title: 26 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report for FY 2018/19 – 2020/21 reveals that machinery accounted for ETB 3.5 billion (9.1%) of total import tax expenditure. Notably, customs duty tax expenditures on animal or vegetable fats significantly increased in FY 2020/21 compared to the previous two fiscal years, while vehicle-related tax expenditures also remained substantial. The report analyzes these trends across those three fiscal years for Ethiopia's import taxes.

child_sections.title: 27 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report for FY 2018/19 – 2020/21 details customs duty tax expenditures categorized by commodity, totaling 798.82 ETB million in FY 2020/21. A significant portion of these expenditures fall under the "Others" category (54.11% in FY 2020/21), while further detailed breakdowns for FY 2018/19 and FY 2019/20 are available elsewhere. The report is presented in Table 4.6, focusing on figures expressed in Ethiopian Birr (ETB) millions.

child_sections.title: 28 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report for FY 2018/19 – 2020/21 details excise duty tax expenditures, showing a fluctuating pattern over the three years. The report breaks down these expenditures into direct effects, indirect effects via VAT, indirect effects through Surtax, and higher-order indirect effects (represented visually in Figure 4.5). While specific values aren't provided in this excerpt, the graph indicates changes in each expenditure category annually.

child_sections.title: 29 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report for FY 2018/19 – 2020/21, as illustrated in Figure 4.6, details excise duty tax expenditures relative to imports, GDP, and overall trade tax spending. These expenditures represented a fluctuating share of imports (around 8-16%) over the three fiscal years, while remaining consistently below 1% of GDP. The report notes these figures assume no double-counting of trade taxes.

child_sections.title: 30 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report for FY 2018/19 – 2020/21, as detailed in Table 4.7, focuses on excise duty tax expenditures measured in Ethiopian Birr (ETB) millions.  Over this period, total excise tax expenditure included direct effects, indirect effects through VAT surtax, and higher-order effects, with capital goods accounting for a significant portion in FY 2018/19 at 30.37 ETB million. The report quantifies these expenditures to understand the financial impact of various tax incentives and exemptions related to imports.

child_sections.title: 31 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report for FY 2018/19 – 2020/21 (Table 4.8) details excise duty tax expenditures broken down by commodity category in Ethiopian Birr (ETB million). It quantifies these expenditures into direct effects, indirect effects via VAT, and higher-order effects resulting from surtaxes. The report analyzes the total excise tax expenditure across broad commodity categories but does not specify which commodities are included within those categories beyond noting their classification.

child_sections.title: 32 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The import tax expenditure report for FY 2018/19 – 2020/21 highlights that tax expenditures on motor vehicle imports reached ETB 23.0 billion (0.5% of GDP) in 2020/21. This figure represents a significant increase—tripling from the previous fiscal year, 2019/20—making motor vehicles a substantial component of Ethiopia's overall tax expenditures. The report focuses on these expenditures within the Ethiopian economy (ETB).

child_sections.title: 33 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The Ethiopian Ministry of Finance's FY 2018/19 – 2020/21 report indicates significant VAT tax expenditures totaling ETB 44.6 billion in FY 2020/21.  Direct VAT forgone accounted for a substantial ETB 42.2 billion (94.7%) of this total, with an additional ETB 2.4 billion (5.3%) lost through surtax effects. These figures represent a considerable impact on government revenue.

child_sections.title: 34 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report for FY 2018/19 – 2020/21, as illustrated in Figure 4.8, details VAT expenditures related to imports. These expenditures fluctuated between approximately 30-45% of total imports over the three fiscal years, representing a notable portion of both import tax revenue and overall GDP. The report analyzes these figures as a share of imports, GDP, and all trade tax expenditure, though specific numerical values are not provided in this excerpt.

child_sections.title: 35 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report for FY 2018/19 – 2020/21 details VAT expenditures in Ethiopia (ETB million) categorized by type—primarily capital/investment goods and those under the Second Schedule. Total VAT expenditure reached 327.40 ETB million in FY 2018/19, increasing to 417.28 ETB million and further to an unspecified amount over the three-year period, with both direct and indirect effects considered through surtax mechanisms. The report is presented in Table 4.9, outlining expenditure breakdowns for each fiscal year.

child_sections.title: 36 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report for FY 2018/19 – 2020/21 details VAT expenditures by commodity category, with data specifically for FY 2020/21 presented in Table 4.10. In that year, total VAT expenditure reached ETB 71.97 million on animal products and ETB 142.71 million on vegetable products, broken down into direct and indirect effects through surtax. The report focuses on quantifying these expenditures across different import commodities within Ethiopia (ETB currency).

child_sections.title: 37 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report for FY 2018/19 – 2020/21 details total tax expenditures of ETB 11 billion, with surtax accounting for ETB 13.5 billion in FY 2018/19 alone. Notably, these surtax expenditures are applied as the final calculation step in border taxes and do not impact the collection of other taxes. The report also includes data visualized in Figure 4.9 regarding surtax expenditure trends over those fiscal years.

child_sections.title: 38 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report for FY 2018/19 – 2020/21, as illustrated in Figure 4.10, details surtax expenditures relative to imports, GDP, and overall trade tax spending. Surtax expenditure as a share of imports decreased from approximately 16% in FY 2018/19 to below 12% in FY 2020/21, while remaining relatively stable as a percentage of GDP during the same period. The report assumes no double-counting of transactions when calculating these figures.

child_sections.title: 39 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report for FY 2018/19 – 2020/21 details surtax expenditures in Ethiopian Birr (ETB) million, revealing significant losses primarily from exemptions categorized as “Non-capital/second schedule” which totaled 11,997.24 ETB million in FY 2018/19 alone. Capital/investment related surtax expenditure remained relatively low across the three fiscal years, while expenditures linked to the "Second Schedule" also contributed substantially to overall losses. The report focuses on direct effects of these surtax exemptions, indicating a considerable financial impact from import-related tax concessions.

child_sections.title: 40 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report for FY 2018/19 – 2020/21 details surtax expenditures across various commodity categories in Ethiopia (ETB million). In FY 2020/21, significant direct effects of surtax expenditure were observed in **animal or vegetable fats** (4,153.68 ETB million), followed by **prepared food and beverages** (158.55 ETB million) and smaller amounts for **animal & vegetable products** (150.97 ETB million). The report is presented in Table 4.12, providing a commodity-specific breakdown of these

child_sections.title: 41 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: This report by the Ethiopian Customs Commission estimates import tax expenditures for Ethiopia across FY 2018/19 – 2020/21 using detailed shipment-level data. The study quantified foregone revenue due to various exemptions and concessions granted on imported goods during those fiscal years. It represents an analysis of government financial impact related to import policies.

child_sections.title: 42 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report for FY 2018/19 – 2020/21 reveals issues with shipment-level microdata used to understand underpayment of import taxes. Specifically, the data currently lacks sufficient detail and quality, hindering comprehensive analysis. Improving data collection—through increased detail, higher quality, and faster availability—is recommended for better reporting on tax expenditure.

child_sections.title: 43 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report for FY 2018/19 – 2020/21 focuses on **positive tax expenditures**, analyzing instances where importers pay reduced customs duties/taxes compared to a standard benchmark system. While the main report examines these reductions, Appendix A details **negative tax expenditures**—cases where importers effectively pay *more* than the benchmark rate. This suggests a comprehensive analysis of deviations from typical import taxation practices is undertaken by the reporting entity (unspecified in this excerpt).

child_sections.title: 44 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report for FY 2018/19 – 2020/21, as shown in Figure A.1, details negative tax expenditures across Customs, Excise VAT, and Surtax categories. These negative values indicate instances where actual taxes collected were *lower* than what would be expected at a standard rate, resulting in a loss of potential revenue. The report shows a generally increasing trend of these losses over the three fiscal years, particularly noticeable for Excise VAT and Surtax.

child_sections.title: 45 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: This report details the indirect effect of excise tax expenditures from FY 2018/19 to 2020/21, utilizing a formula to calculate these effects through Value Added Tax (VAT) and surtax mechanisms. The calculation considers differences in tax rates (`𝜏𝑏𝑒` vs `𝜏𝑎𝑒`) applied to various components like base value (`𝜏𝑏𝑣`), sales (`𝜏𝑏𝑠`), and a composite factor (`1+𝜏𝑎𝑐`) impacting Capital Investment Funds (CIF).  Essentially, it quantifies how excise tax changes ripple through the economy via related taxes.

child_sections.title: 46 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report for FY 2018/19 – 2020/21 analyzes customs duty and excise tax expenditures. The report, visually represented in Figure C.1, shows fluctuations in both types of expenditures over the three fiscal years, with a general trend of variability rather than consistent growth or decline. While specific values aren't provided, the graph illustrates that **Customs Duty expenditure was consistently higher than Excise Tax expenditure** across the period examined.

child_sections.title: 47 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report for FY 2018/19 – 2020/21 reveals that import tax collected through the eCMS system represented 95% of actual collection, while combined data from all IT systems exceeded actual collection by 7%. This discrepancy suggests significant overlap in shipment recording between different IT systems—likely including both eCMS and another unspecified system—leading to double-counting.

child_sections.title: 48 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report for FY 2018/19 – 2020/21 analyzes tax expenditures reported through two IT systems, **eCMS** and **ASYCUDA**. The report shows a general decline in total tax expenditure over the three fiscal years, with **eCMS** consistently reporting higher values than **ASYCUDA**. Figure D.2 visually represents this trend, illustrating the amounts of tax expenditure recorded by each system annually from 2018/19 to 2020/21.

child_sections.title: 49 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report for FY 2018/19 – 2020/21 categorizes imported commodities using Harmonized System (HS) codes, specifically chapters 1-23. These categories broadly include animal products (1-5), vegetable products (6-14), fats (15), and prepared foods & beverages including sugar (16-23). The report likely analyzes tax revenue foregone due to concessions or exemptions applied to these commodity groups, though specific financial details aren't provided in this excerpt.

child_sections.title: 50 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report for FY 2018/19 – 2020/21 details tax expenditures across several product categories, with **Plastics and rubbers** and **Other chemical products** representing the largest values at $1,163.41 and $1,178.69 respectively over the period. **Pharmaceuticals** also had significant expenditure ($153.50), while **Fertilizer**, **Leather and shoes** showed comparatively lower figures. The report provides a breakdown of these expenditures across the three fiscal years, indicating fluctuations within each category.

child_sections.title: 51 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report for FY 2018/19 – 2020/21 details tax revenue foregone on various imported goods, totaling **$899.07** across the three fiscal years. Significant expenditure categories include **Mineral Oil & Fuel ($247.46)**, **Other Mineral Products (including cement) ($448.53)** and **Plastics and Rubbers ($1119.03)**, while **Pharmaceuticals ($150.11)** also represent a notable portion of the total. Notably, no tax expenditure was reported for **Fertiliser** during this period.

child_sections.title: 52 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report for FY 2018/19 – 2020/21 details tax expenditures primarily on **Vehicles** (reaching a total of 3,031.30) and **Other Machinery** (totaling 24.30). Significant amounts were also reported for **Others** category with a total of 269.95, while categories like **Aircraft**, **Furniture**, **Animal products**, and **Vegetable products** showed no recorded tax expenditure during this period. The figures represent values likely in millions (currency unspecified) across the three fiscal years examined.

child_sections.title: 53 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report for FY 2018/19 – 2020/21 details tax expenditures primarily on **vehicles**, totaling $12,930.38 over the period, with significant amounts reported in FY 2018/19 ($10,514.53). Minimal to no tax expenditure was recorded for **aircraft, other transport equipment, and furniture** during this timeframe; a small amount ($40.92 total) was noted under an "Others" category. The report acknowledges potential limitations due to the possibility of duplicate transaction reporting across customs systems.

child_sections.title: 54 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report for FY 2018/19 – 2020/21 details tax revenue foregone on various imported goods, totaling significant amounts across key sectors. **Base metals and articles of base metal** represented the largest expenditure at $2,495.12 (up from $2,272.33), followed by **other chemical products** at $1,160.01. Other notable expenditures were reported for **plastics & rubbers**, **textiles**, and **paper & wood products**.

child_sections.title: 55 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report for FY 2018/19 – 2020/21 details tax revenue foregone due to import duty exemptions or concessions on specific goods. **Animal/vegetable fats** accounted for the largest expenditure at 1,351.00, followed by **prepared food and beverages (including sugar)** at 932.60; other significant expenditures were noted for **mineral oil & fuel** (259.47) and **plastics & rubbers** (254.94). Notably, there was no reported tax expenditure for **pharmaceuticals** during this period.

child_sections.title: 56 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report for FY 2018/19 – 2020/21 details tax revenue foregone due to import duty exemptions or concessions on various goods. **Base metals and articles of base metal** accounted for the largest expenditure at 1,936.57 (currency unspecified), followed by **textiles** (702.63) and **other chemical products** (448.86). Other significant expenditures were noted for **plastics & rubbers** (340.48) and **paper & wood products** (115.84), with minimal to no reported expenditure for **pharm

child_sections.title: 57 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report for FY 2018/19 – 2020/21 details tax concessions granted on various imported goods, with **base metals**, **electrical machinery**, and **other machinery** consistently representing the largest share of these expenditures. While expenditure varied across fiscal years, **vehicles** saw a significant decrease in concessions from FY18/19 to FY20/21, while **aircraft** experienced an increase during that period. The report provides percentage breakdowns for each category over the three-year timeframe, indicating shifting patterns in import tax relief.

child_sections.title: 58 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report for FY 2018/19 – 2020/21 details tax revenue foregone on various imported goods. Key categories with notable expenditure include “Other chemical products” consistently around 3%, and increasing expenditures on "Pharmaceuticals" (rising to 2.9% in 2020/21) and "Fertiliser" (reaching 3.0% in 2020/21). Other categories like plastics, rubbers, cement and leather represent smaller portions of overall import tax expenditure.

child_sections.title: 59 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The Import Tax Expenditure Report for FY 2018/19 – 2020/21 focuses on tax expenditures related to import duties governed by Council of Ministers Regulations No. 133/2007 (Import Sur-Tax Regulation) and No. 270/2012 (Investment Incentives). These regulations outline exemptions or reductions in import taxes, likely impacting government revenue through incentives for investment and specific reserved areas. The report itself doesn't contain substantive findings within this excerpt – it primarily lists the legal frameworks under which these expenditures operate.

child_sections.title: 60 Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21
child_sections.summary: The “Import Tax Expenditure Report: FY 2018/19 – 2020/21” is based on legal frameworks defined by Proclamation No. 859/2014 (Customs Proclamation) and Proclamation No. 886/2015 (Industrial Park Proclamation). These proclamations govern import tax expenditures within Ethiopia during the specified fiscal years. The report likely analyzes revenue foregone due to various exemptions or incentives outlined in these laws, though specific details aren't provided in this excerpt.

summary: Top-level page index over extracted sections
